title: The Mirror of the Sea
description: The Mirror of the Sea — knowledge fragments for AI agents

memes.label: About This Wiki
memes.description: A knowledge wiki for "The Mirror of the Sea" — this page is auto-generated from book metadata

memes.label: Table of Contents
memes.description: Table of Contents — The Mirror of the Sea by Joseph Conrad

relations.label: The Weight of the Burden — XIII
The Weight of the Burden — XIV
The Weight of the Burden — XV
The Grip of the Land — XX
The Grip of the Land — XXI
The Character of the Foe — XXII
The Character of the Foe — XXIII
The Character of the Foe — XXIV
Rulers of East and West — XXV
Rulers of East and West — XXVI
Rulers of East and West — XXVII
Rulers of East and West — XXVIII
Rulers of East and West — XXIX
The Nursery of the Craft — XXXVII
The Nursery of the Craft — XXXVIII
The Nursery of the Craft — XXXIX

memes.label: Agulhas Bank
memes.description: The shallow fishing grounds off the southern coast of Africa, where the Agulhas Current runs and where Conrad knew the West Wind could be openly defied — a place where a ship homeward bound from the East Indies met the full force of the southern ocean.

memes.label: Ahead
memes.description: The direction directly in front of a vessel's bow; in Conrad's maritime world, the direction towards which the commander and watch officer gaze with steady, searching attention.

memes.label: Albert Docks
memes.description: The great dock complex on the north bank of the Thames at Gallions Reach, marking the downstream limit of Conrad's "virgin forest" of waterside buildings — the furthest reach of the London dock system before the isolation of Tilbury.

memes.label: Aloft
memes.description: The upper region of a ship's masts, spars, and rigging, above the deck, where seamen work the sails and where the ship first becomes visible from afar.

memes.label: The Amsterdam Loading Incident
memes.description: Conrad's first experience as chief mate — loading a general cargo at Amsterdam in winter, where frozen canals, impatient owners, and a fatal miscalculation in stowage taught him the perilous art of loading a sailing-ship.

memes.label: Anchor
memes.description: The forged iron device that secures a ship to the sea-bottom — in Conrad's maritime philosophy, the heaviest burden of the seaman's daily duties and a symbol of hope, the tangible link between the floating world and the solid earth.

memes.label: Anchoring
memes.description: The art and ritual of anchoring — the beginning and end of every passage, and the heaviest responsibility of the seaman's working life.

relations.label: The Weight of the Burden — XIII
The Weight of the Burden — XIV
The Weight of the Burden — XV

memes.label: Astern
memes.description: The direction behind a vessel — in Conrad's memoir, the world left behind, the place where chasers appear, the bearing of a threatening sky, and the surge of a following sea.

memes.label: Atlantic Ocean
memes.description: The Atlantic Ocean as Conrad experienced it — the domain of the West Wind, whose "battalions of Atlantic rollers" assault the shores of Europe, and the great theatre of the sailing ship's struggle with elemental forces.

memes.label: "Author’s Note"
memes.description: "Less perhaps than any other book written by me, or anybody else, does this volume require a preface. Yet since all the others including even the *Personal Record*, which is but a fragment of biography..."

memes.label: Balancelle
memes.description: A small Mediterranean sailing vessel with two short raking masts and enormous lateen sails; Conrad sailed one as a young man in the Gulf of Lions, and describes it as the "cradle" of his sea-life.

memes.label: Ballast
memes.description: The weight carried low in a ship to give her stability — and the subject of one of Conrad's most pointed observations on the gap between a shipowner's boast and a ship's actual behaviour at sea.

memes.label: Barcelona
memes.description: The Barcelona of Conrad's Tremolino days — a Mediterranean port where the syndicate maintained a villa on the Prado and from whose harbour the tartane slipped out on her final, fatal voyage.

memes.label: Barometer
memes.description: The nautical instrument for measuring atmospheric pressure — which, as Conrad observes, gives no warning of an Easterly gale however wet it may be.

memes.label: Barque
memes.description: A sailing vessel with three or more masts, square-rigged on the fore and main but fore-and-aft rigged on the mizzen; a workhorse of the nineteenth-century carrying trade, appearing throughout Conrad's maritime memoirs.

memes.label: Battle of Trafalgar
memes.description: The Battle of Trafalgar (21 October 1805) — the culminating naval engagement of the Napoleonic Wars, in which Lord Nelson's fleet destroyed the combined French and Spanish fleets off Cape Trafalgar, and Nelson himself fell in the hour of victory. For Conrad, Trafalgar was the supreme expression of the naval spirit and the heroic age.

memes.label: Bearing
memes.description: The navigational practice of taking bearings—angular measurements relative to landmarks or celestial bodies—to determine a ship's position at sea.

memes.label: Boatswain
memes.description: The warrant officer responsible for the ship's rigging, anchors, and deck crew--a voice of command during the critical approach to land.

memes.label: Bow
memes.description: The forward part of a ship's hull, the part that meets the waves first and whose lines define a vessel's character and speed.

memes.label: Breeze
memes.description: The moderate wind that fills a sailing ship's canvas without threatening its spars — the most welcome of all weather conditions to the seaman, watched and waited for with almost prayerful attention.

memes.label: Brig
memes.description: A two-masted sailing vessel, square-rigged on both masts; a workhorse of nineteenth-century maritime trade, and a familiar sight in Conrad's sea-narratives of the wool fleet and coastal waters.

memes.label: Brigantine
memes.description: The brigantine is a two-masted sailing vessel, square-rigged on the foremast and fore-and-aft rigged on the mainmast. Compact, weatherly, and handsome, the brigantine earned Conrad's open admiration as one of the prettiest rigs afloat and a capable sea-boat for deep-water passages.

memes.label: Bristol Channel
memes.description: The sea-approach to Bristol, famous in Conrad's era for the copper-ore trade — a route worked by "the staunchest ships afloat" and regarded as the school of staunch seamen.

memes.label: Bulwark
memes.description: The raised side of a ship above the deck, providing shelter from seas and preventing crew from being washed overboard.

memes.label: Buoy
memes.description: A floating marker anchored to the seabed, used for navigation, mooring, or as a life-saving device.

memes.label: Cabin
memes.description: The captain's and officers' quarters aboard a sailing ship, at once a place of command, solitude, and refuge.

memes.label: Cable
memes.description: The heavy rope or chain by which a ship rides at anchor — in Conrad's prose, a thing that "grows" under strain, taut as a bowstring, and the special charge of the chief mate.

memes.label: Calcutta
memes.description: The great port city of British India on the Hooghly River, a regular destination for the merchant sailing ships of Conrad's era — the source of jute cargoes and the setting for shipboard talk of ground-tackle and river pilotage.

memes.label: Cape Horn
memes.description: The southernmost headland of the Americas — Cape Horn, whose gales and bright-moonlight wildness Conrad describes as the most terrible and beautiful weather a seaman can endure, and whose rounding was the ultimate test of a sailing ship.

memes.label: Cape of Good Hope
memes.description: The great southern cape of Africa, known to Portuguese navigators as the Cape of Storms and to Conrad as the proving ground of seamanship — a place where the West Wind's power is felt at its most magnificent and most terrifying.

memes.label: Captain MacW——
memes.description: A shipmaster under whom Conrad served as chief mate on his first voyage — Captain MacW—— was a reclusive commander who remained shut in his cabin, a figure Conrad recalls with "a certain flavour of dislike" but also professional understanding.

memes.label: Captain S——
memes.description: A shipmaster under whom Conrad served aboard the wool-clipper Tweed — Captain S—— believed his personal presence and "right feeling" were the secret to a ship's good performance, a conviction Conrad treats with affectionate irony while acknowledging the deeper truth it contained about command at sea.

memes.label: Cargo
memes.description: The commercial purpose of the merchant ship — cargo handling as the defining rhythm of port life, and the loading and unloading that bookend every sea passage.

relations.label: The Nursery of the Craft — XXXVII

memes.label: The Carlist Syndicate
memes.description: The four-member Carlist syndicate that owned and operated the tartane Tremolino — a clandestine political and smuggling operation based in Barcelona and Marseille, whose members included Doña Rita, J. M. K. B., and the young Joseph Conrad.

memes.label: Maritime Ceremony
memes.description: The formal observances — from the ritual of departure to the pageantry of naval progress — that invest maritime life with meaning and mark its significant transitions.

memes.label: Cesar Cervoni
memes.description: Dominic Cervoni's nephew — a wretched, cadaverous seaman whose odious character, chronic insubordination, and mysterious shore absences nearly undid the Tremolino's final voyage.

memes.label: Chain
memes.description: The heavy iron chain cable connecting a ship's anchor to the vessel, whose thunderous rumble marks the close of a passage.

memes.label: Channel
memes.description: A navigable waterway — in Conrad's world, most often the English Channel, the narrow sea-gate through which homeward-bound ships must pass, a place of heightened alertness and final approach.

memes.label: Chart
memes.description: The nautical chart, the seaman's primary navigational tool—a map of the sea that translates bearings and soundings into safe passage.

memes.label: Chief Mate
memes.description: The chief mate in Conrad's maritime world — the ship's second-in-command, keeper of anchors and guardian of cables, whose domain is the forward part of the vessel and whose responsibilities bridge the gap between the master and the crew.

memes.label: Cicero
memes.description: A barque laid up at Sydney's Circular Quay — the Cicero appears in Conrad's memoir as the ship of Mr. B——'s crony, the setting for one of the most comic and tender vignettes in The Mirror of the Sea, a nightly ritual of friendship enacted between two captive ships.

memes.label: Circular Quay
memes.description: The heart of Sydney's harbour and the centre of maritime life in colonial Australia, where the great wool-clippers lay for months loading their cargoes and where Conrad observed the last flowering of the sailing-ship era.

memes.label: Clipper
memes.description: The fast sailing ship of the nineteenth century — the culminating expression of maritime beauty and speed before steam supplanted sail; the wool-clippers of the Clyde and their swan-song era in the London and Sydney docks.

memes.label: Coasting
memes.description: The art of navigating along a coastline — a distinct discipline requiring intimate knowledge of local waters, tides, and landmarks, and the foundation of the maritime trade that fed the great ocean routes.

relations.label: The Grip of the Land — XX

memes.label: "Cobwebs and Gossamer — X"
memes.description: "From the main truck of the average tall ship the horizon describes a circle of many miles, in which you can see another ship right down to her waterline; and these very eyes which follow this writing"

memes.label: "Cobwebs and Gossamer — XI"
memes.description: "Indeed, it is less than nothing, and I have seen, when the great soul of the world turned over with a heavy sigh, a perfectly new, extra-stout foresail vanish like a bit of some airy stuff much lighte..."

memes.label: "Cobwebs and Gossamer — XII"
memes.description: "So sail did get shortened more or less in time even in that ship, and her tall spars never went overboard while I served in her. However, all the time I was with them, Captain S—— and Mr. P—— did not"

memes.label: "Colophon"
memes.description: "Chapter of The Mirror of the Sea"

memes.label: Compass
memes.description: The navigational instrument by which a ship determines her heading; Conrad uses it as a metaphor for the fixed point of reference a seaman carries in his mind through the trackless sea.

memes.label: Index of Concepts
memes.description: A comprehensive index of all maritime and nautical concept pages in The Mirror of the Sea knowledge base, sortable by label and reference count.

memes.label: The Cook
memes.description: The ship's cook, a ubiquitous figure in Conrad's maritime world who presides over the galley and appears in moments of crisis — from soup complaints to derelict rescues.

memes.label: Corsica
memes.description: The Mediterranean island that produced Dominic Cervoni — a land of vendettas, maquis, and "good sturdy flesh and blood," whose fierce maritime culture shaped the men who sailed its waters.

memes.label: Course
memes.description: The compass direction a ship steers; in Conrad's memoir, the course is both a practical navigation term and a metaphor for the trajectory of a voyage and a life.

memes.label: Craft
memes.description: Conrad's conception of ships as individual entities — each craft possesses a distinctive character to which the seaman must adapt, for the ship, made by man, is one with man.

relations.label: The Nursery of the Craft — XXXVII
The Grip of the Land — XX

memes.label: Crew
memes.description: The collective body of men who work a ship, united by mutual dependence and a fierce loyalty to their vessel despite all hardships — the living soul of the ship in Conrad's maritime philosophy.

memes.label: Cutter
memes.description: The racing rig par excellence among fore-and-aft vessels--Conrad's typology of sailing craft in The Mirror of the Sea assigns the cutter the most imposing appearance and the purpose of speed.

memes.label: Deck
memes.description: The deck as the working platform of a ship — the stage upon which the seaman's life and duties unfold, weathering gales, receiving departing captains, and bearing the weight of a ship's daily drama.

memes.label: "Dedication"
memes.description: "To Mrs. Katherine Sanderson whose warm welcome and gracious hospitality extended to the friend of her son cheered the first dark days of my parting with the sea these pages are affectionately inscribe..."

memes.label: Departure
memes.description: The ceremonial last professional sight of land, marking the beginning of an ocean passage — the complementary ritual to the Landfall.

memes.label: Dock
memes.description: The enclosed basin where ships lie fettered to rigid shores, a scene of both quotidian labour and historical glamour along the Thames.

memes.label: Dominic Cervoni
memes.description: Corsican seaman and padrone of the tartane Tremolino — Conrad's trusted comrade in the Mediterranean smuggling enterprise, a man of astute ruthlessness and fierce loyalty.

memes.label: Don Carlos (Carlist Pretender)
memes.description: The Carlist pretender to the Spanish throne — Don Carlos was the remote and semi-legendary figure around whom the Tremolino syndicate's Royalist politics revolved, a man Conrad treats as a presence felt rather than a person known.

memes.label: Doña Rita
memes.description: The enigmatic Doña Rita — intelligence source, syndicate hostess, and the social pivot of the Tremolino smuggling enterprise, whose "wit and wisdom and information" Conrad credits as indispensable to the operation.

memes.label: The Downs
memes.description: The great roadstead off the Kentish coast between the North Foreland and the South Foreland — a traditional anchorage where deep-water ships paused before entering the Thames or awaited favourable winds for departure.

memes.label: East Wind
memes.description: The prevailing wind of the tropics and the interloper of high latitudes — personified by Conrad as a cold, crafty autocrat whose duplicity makes him the most treacherous of the ruling winds.

memes.label: Easterly Weather
memes.description: The East Wind in Conrad's maritime cosmology — a treacherous, tyrannical force ruling the easterly latitudes with cold persistence and diabolical cunning, contrasted against the honourable West Wind in the symbolic geography of The Mirror of the Sea.

memes.label: English Channel
memes.description: The English Channel as the seaman's gateway — the narrow sea between England and France where Atlantic weather meets the home shore, and where every homeward-bound ship enters the final, most dangerous phase of her passage.

memes.label: "Epigraph 1"
memes.description: "Chapter of The Mirror of the Sea"

memes.label: Essex Coast
memes.description: The low, flat shoreline on the northern side of the Thames Estuary — a landscape of marshes, mudflats, and desolate red-brick edifices that Conrad observed on every passage up the river.

memes.label: Index of Events
memes.description: An index of all events in The Mirror of the Sea — voyages, storms, rescues, and naval battles that punctuate Conrad's maritime memoir.

memes.label: Fathom
memes.description: The traditional unit of depth measurement at sea — six feet — and the act of sounding by which the mariner reads the sea floor to determine his position and the safety of his anchorage.

memes.label: Featured Entities
memes.description: A curated gallery of featured and premium-quality entity pages in The Mirror of the Sea — the flagship content representing the highest standard of completeness and citation.

memes.label: Figurehead
memes.description: The carved figure mounted on a sailing ship's prow; in Conrad's London docks, a "serried rank" of magnificent figureheads formed an open-air gallery of an art now vanished from the sea.

memes.label: Fine Art of Sailing
memes.description: Conrad's philosophical treatise on sailing as one of the fine arts — a pursuit requiring single-minded sincerity, intimate knowledge of each craft's character, and ceaseless striving toward perfection.

relations.label: The Nursery of the Craft — XXXVII
The Nursery of the Craft — XXXVIII
The Nursery of the Craft — XXXIX

memes.label: Fishing-Smack
memes.description: The smallest of sea-going craft, the fishing-smack represents the humblest vessel that must contend with the same winds and seas as the great ships — a measure of both the sea's democracy and its indifference to scale.

memes.label: Fog
memes.description: The grey obliteration of the visible world at sea — fog as the seaman's most disorienting adversary, erasing all landmarks and reducing navigation to a state of blind faith.

relations.label: The Character of the Foe — XXIV

memes.label: Forecastle
memes.description: The forward part of a ship's upper deck, traditionally the living quarters of the crew and the station for anchoring and lookout duties — a space of both work and rest in Conrad's maritime world.

memes.label: Foremast
memes.description: The forwardmost mast of a sailing ship — the centre of the chief mate's domain, where anchors are stowed, men are mustered, and the working life of the vessel is concentrated.

memes.label: Foresail
memes.description: The principal square sail set on the foremast of a sailing ship — a driver of speed and, in Conrad's account, a sail often sacrificed to the fury of the wind.

memes.label: Gale
memes.description: The gale as a living adversary at sea — Conrad's philosophical and experiential account of the personalities of great winds and the seaman's relationship with them, from the silent gale to the moonlit terror off Cape Horn.

relations.label: The Character of the Foe — XXII
The Character of the Foe — XXIII
The Character of the Foe — XXIV

memes.label: The Gale off Cape Horn
memes.description: The most vividly remembered gale in The Mirror of the Sea — a storm off Cape Horn under bright moonlight that Conrad recalled decades later as "a true expression of dishevelled wildness," an event whose peculiar character was stamped into memory by the phrase of a shipmate he did not even like.

memes.label: Galley
memes.description: An oar-propelled vessel of antiquity and the Mediterranean; Conrad reflects on the Roman galley that first explored the Thames estuary and on the galley's endurance through naval history.

memes.label: Gangway
memes.description: The narrow, springy plank connecting a moored ship to the shore, a threshold between shipboard life and the land.

memes.label: Harbour-Master
memes.description: The port official responsible for the berthing and safety of ships in harbour — a figure Conrad casts as a watchful, brass-buttoned gaoler of captive vessels, a renegade seaman who has abandoned the sea for the tyranny of the quay.

memes.label: Hatchway
memes.description: An opening in the deck of a ship providing access to the cargo holds and interior spaces below.

memes.label: Hawser
memes.description: A heavy rope used for mooring, towing, or warping a vessel; in Conrad's dockside world, the hawser is the bond that ties the free ship to the "strong, muddy, enslaved earth."

memes.label: Helm
memes.description: The instrument by which a ship is steered — and the seat of command where the relationship between man, ship, and sea finds its most immediate expression.

memes.label: Hold
memes.description: The below-deck compartment of a ship where cargo is stowed; in Conrad's memoir, a space of labour, refuge, and the theatre of shipboard life below the waterline.

memes.label: Hull
memes.description: The watertight body of a ship, excluding masts, sails, rigging, and superstructure.

memes.label: Hurricane
memes.description: The tropical revolving storm of extraordinary violence, whose approach is heralded by a peculiar olive-hued cloud — one of the most feared phenomena in the seaman's world.

memes.label: Hyperion
memes.description: A barque laid up in the London docks — the Hyperion appears in Conrad's meditation on captive ships, her "aristocratic" presence among the docked vessels a reminder that even the finest ships lose their dignity when removed from the sea.

memes.label: J. M. K. B.
memes.description: The "Américain, Catholique, et gentilhomme" — J. M. K. B. was a member of the Tremolino syndicate, an elegantly fatal American Royalist who haunted the margins of Conrad's Mediterranean adventure.

memes.label: Jib
memes.description: The jib is a triangular sail set forward of the foremast, flown from the jibboom in the head-rig of a sailing vessel. It is a powerful steering sail, critical to balancing the ship's head and driving her to windward.

memes.label: Joseph Conrad
memes.description: Joseph Conrad (1857–1924), the author of *The Mirror of the Sea*; a Polish-born British seaman turned writer whose two decades at sea inform every page of his maritime memoir — "a full confession not of my sins but of my emotions."

memes.label: Keel
memes.description: The ship's backbone — the central structural timber or bar running along the bottom of the hull from bow to stern, whose contact with the sea-bed marks for Conrad the difference between a living ship and a stranded wreck.

memes.label: Knot
memes.description: A unit of speed equal to one nautical mile per hour, used in maritime and aviation contexts.

memes.label: Landfall
memes.description: The moment a ship first sights land after a sea passage — a critical event in navigation and a defining rhythm of a seaman's life.

memes.label: "Landfalls and Departures — I"
memes.description: "Landfall and Departure mark the rhythmical swing of a seaman’s life and of a ship’s career. From land to land is the most concise definition of a ship’s earthly fate. A “Departure” is not what a vain"

memes.label: "Landfalls and Departures — II"
memes.description: "Some commanders of ships take their Departure from the home coast sadly, in a spirit of grief and discontent. They have a wife, children perhaps, some affection at any rate, or perhaps only some pet v..."

memes.label: "Landfalls and Departures — III"
memes.description: "Quite another case, and having nothing to do with drink, was that of poor Captain B——. He used to suffer from sick headaches, in his young days, every time he was approaching a coast. Well over fifty"

memes.label: "Landfalls and Departures — IV"
memes.description: "Before an anchor can ever be raised, it must be let go; and this perfectly obvious truism brings me at once to the subject of the degradation of the sea language in the daily press of this country. Yo..."

memes.label: "Landfalls and Departures — IX"
memes.description: "Every passage of a ship of yesterday, whose yards were braced round eagerly the very moment the pilot, with his pockets full of letters, had got over the side, was like a race—a race against time, aga..."

memes.label: "Landfalls and Departures — V"
memes.description: "From first to last the seaman’s thoughts are very much concerned with his anchors. It is not so much that the anchor is a symbol of hope as that it is the heaviest object that he has to handle on boar..."

memes.label: "Landfalls and Departures — VI"
memes.description: "It is difficult to disconnect the idea of ships’ anchors from the idea of the ship’s chief mate—the man who sees them go down clear and come up sometimes foul; because not even the most unremitting ca..."

memes.label: "Landfalls and Departures — VII"
memes.description: "The other year, looking through a newspaper of sound principles, but whose staff *will* persist in “casting” anchors and going to sea “on” a ship (ough!), I came across an article upon the season’s ya..."

memes.label: "Landfalls and Departures — VIII"
memes.description: "The sailing and racing of yachts has developed a class of fore-and-aft sailors, men born and bred to the sea, fishing in winter and yachting in summer; men to whom the handling of that particular rig"

memes.label: Latitude
memes.description: The angular distance of a point on the earth's surface north or south of the equator — and, in the seaman's world, the measure of a region's weather, daylight, and temper.

memes.label: Lee
memes.description: The side or direction sheltered from the wind — and, in its most menacing form, the lee shore upon which a sailing vessel may be driven to her destruction.

memes.label: Lee Shore
memes.description: The coast towards which the wind is blowing--in The Mirror of the Sea, the ultimate peril that the Easterly gale threatens, making a seaman "feel blind for life."

memes.label: Leeward
memes.description: The side of a vessel sheltered from the wind; the downwind direction towards which a sailing ship heels and the sea runs.

memes.label: Lighter
memes.description: A flat-bottomed barge used to load and discharge cargo from ships at anchor or alongside quays — a humble vessel that Conrad elevates, fleetingly, into a symbol of how the sea's indifference dwarfs all human industry.

memes.label: Lightship
memes.description: A permanently anchored vessel serving as a floating lighthouse, marking shoals and navigational hazards at harbour approaches where a fixed lighthouse cannot be built.

memes.label: Loading
memes.description: The art and science of loading a sailing ship — a task that determined a vessel's speed, safety, and behaviour at sea, and which Conrad presents as a lost skill of the great age of sail.

memes.label: London
memes.description: London as seen from the sea — Conrad's portrait of the great port as a place of arrival, departure, and the strange juxtaposition of maritime life with the city's immense commercial sprawl, where the Thames becomes a "virgin forest" of docks, wharves, and the ghosts of ships.

memes.label: London Bridge
memes.description: The historic bridge marking the upper limit of the Port of London's sea-going traffic — the point where, in Conrad's vision, the sea surrenders the river to "the needs, the artifices, the contrivances of toiling men."

memes.label: London Docks
memes.description: The historic dock complex on the north bank of the Thames east of the Tower, celebrated by Conrad for its "old-world air," its aroma of spices, and its far-famed wine-cellars — one of the great engineering achievements of early nineteenth-century London.

memes.label: Lookout
memes.description: The duty of watching for hazards, other vessels, and land from a ship's deck — a vigil central to safety at sea and to the rhythm of the night watches.

memes.label: Lord Nelson
memes.description: Vice-Admiral Horatio, Lord Nelson — the British naval commander whose heroic spirit and moral greatness Conrad invokes in The Mirror of the Sea as the ultimate exemplar of the sea-officer's vocation, the man who "exalted" rather than merely "augmented" the tradition of British naval heroism.

memes.label: Lower Hope Reach
memes.description: A distinctive bend in the Thames between Gravesend and the sea, where the river makes a "slight turn" and — in Conrad's symbolic geography — the soul of the sea abandons the ship and surrenders it to the grasp of the river.

memes.label: Mast
memes.description: The tall vertical spar that carries sails, yards, and rigging on a sailing vessel — in Conrad's rendering, the "pillars" that capture the invisible power of the wind and the very embodiment of the ship's vitality.

memes.label: Master
memes.description: The ship's master in Conrad's maritime world — the commanding figure who embodies the fusion of practical seamanship, moral authority, and artistic dedication to the vessel under his charge.

memes.label: Masthead
memes.description: The highest point of a ship's mast — a vantage for lookouts, a measure of the vessel's presence in harbour, and a symbol of the ship's living connection to sky and horizon.

memes.label: Mate
memes.description: The ship's officer who assists the master — the chief mate is, in Conrad's portrait, the keeper of the anchor, the guardian of the cable, and the practical executor of the ship's routine.

memes.label: Mediterranean Sea
memes.description: The "cradle of oversea traffic" — Conrad's beloved Mediterranean, the inland sea of antiquity whose tideless waters, steep shores, and ancient associations make it, in his view, the most romantic of all seas.

memes.label: Mist
memes.description: The thin, silvery vapour that softens the boundary between sea and sky — a gentler cousin to fog, but no less capable of transforming the seascape into a realm of mystery.

memes.label: Mooring
memes.description: The system of chains, cables, ropes, and posts by which a ship is secured to the shore or to a fixed buoy — the fetters that bind a free vessel to the enslaved earth.

memes.label: Most Referenced Entities
memes.description: A ranked listing of the fifty most-referenced entity pages in The Mirror of the Sea knowledge base, ordered by total backlink count across all pages.

memes.label: Mr. B—— (Chief Mate)
memes.description: Chief mate of a ship laid up in Sydney's Circular Quay — Conrad's reluctant night-watchman companion, whose nocturnal habits provided one of the most vivid character sketches in The Mirror of the Sea.

memes.label: Mr. Hudig (Shipowner)
memes.description: A Dutch shipowner and merchant — Mr. Hudig appears briefly in The Mirror of the Sea as the owner of the ship whose frozen cargo Conrad was sent to collect from a canal-locked port in the depths of a continental winter.

memes.label: Mr. P—— (Hard-of-Hearing Seaman)
memes.description: A young, smart, and profoundly hard-of-hearing seaman — Mr. P—— served on the same ship as Conrad and became notorious for the night-time spectacle of his being shouted at through the roaring dark by an exasperating officer.

memes.label: Naval Architecture
memes.description: The art and science of ship design and construction—in Conrad's era, the age of the iron sailing ship, when shipbuilding attained its highest expression as both industry and art.

memes.label: Navigation
memes.description: The art and science of finding a ship's position and course at sea — a discipline that, in Conrad's hands, becomes a meditation on precision, responsibility, and the relationship between theory and practice.

relations.label: The Nursery of the Craft — XXXIX

memes.label: Nelson's Admirals (Hood, Keith & Hyde Parker)
memes.description: The senior admirals under whom Nelson served — Lord Hood, Admiral Keith, and Sir Hyde Parker — figures Conrad invokes as the institutional framework within which Nelson's individual genius operated.

memes.label: New South Dock
memes.description: The New South Dock on the Thames — Conrad's picture of a dock where ships were laid up, repaired, and stripped of their seagoing dignity by dockmasters, riggers, and the "swarm of renegades" who serve captive vessels.

memes.label: New Zealand
memes.description: A distant British colony and regular port of call for merchant sailing ships in the late nineteenth century, marking the furthest reach of the imperial shipping routes that Conrad knew as a seaman.

memes.label: The Nore Mutiny (1797)
memes.description: The fleet mutiny of May–June 1797 at the Nore anchorage in the Thames Estuary — a mass refusal of duty by sailors of the Royal Navy's North Sea fleet that Conrad invokes as one of the great maritime events haunting the Nore's "historical spot" of "watch and ward kept upon the great throbbing heart of the State."

memes.label: North Atlantic
memes.description: The great ocean of the northern hemisphere, ruled masterfully by the West Wind — the heart of his empire, "most thickly populated with generations of fine ships and hardy men."

memes.label: North Foreland
memes.description: The chalk headland on the Kent coast marking the southern entrance to the Thames Estuary — the first English landmark seen by ships approaching from the south, and a seamark known to mariners since Roman times.

memes.label: North Sea
memes.description: The grey northern sea whose winds are "small princes" in Conrad's hierarchy — the cold, treacherous training ground of North Sea pilots and the sea that shaped the character of northern European seamanship, where the West Wind keeps its turbulent watch over Britain's eastern marches.

memes.label: Oar
memes.description: A long pole with a flat blade used to row or steer a small boat through the water.

memes.label: Index of Organizations
memes.description: An index of all maritime organisations in The Mirror of the Sea — from ancient guilds to modern regulatory authorities that shaped Conrad's seafaring world.

memes.label: Overboard
memes.description: The act of casting or falling into the sea from a vessel — a landsman's misunderstanding of anchoring, and a constant peril of seafaring life that Conrad renders with both dark humour and genuine dread.

memes.label: Overdue and Missing Vessels
memes.description: The shipping-intelligence categories of "overdue" and "missing" — the two words that mark the boundary between hope and despair in maritime life, as explored by Conrad in The Mirror of the Sea.

memes.label: "Overdue and Missing — XIX"
memes.description: "The efficiency of a steamship consists not so much in her courage as in the power she carries within herself. It beats and throbs like a pulsating heart within her iron ribs, and when it stops, the st..."

memes.label: "Overdue and Missing — XVI"
memes.description: "Often I turn with melancholy eagerness to the space reserved in the newspapers under the general heading of “Shipping Intelligence.” I meet there the names of ships I have known. Every year some of th..."

memes.label: "Overdue and Missing — XVII"
memes.description: "The unholy fascination of dread dwells in the thought of the last moments of a ship reported as “missing” in the columns of the *Shipping Gazette*. Nothing of her ever comes to light—no grating, no li..."

memes.label: "Overdue and Missing — XVIII"
memes.description: "But if the word “missing” brings all hope to an end and settles the loss of the underwriters, the word “overdue” confirms the fears already born in many homes ashore, and opens the door of speculation..."

memes.label: Owers
memes.description: The Owers lightship station marks the Owers Bank in the English Channel, a crucial navigational waypoint for vessels traversing the Channel approaches. Conrad invokes it in his meditation on the West Wind's dominion over the seas surrounding the British Isles.

memes.label: Passage
memes.description: In maritime usage, a passage is the completed voyage of a ship from one port to another—a measure of both distance traversed and professional skill exercised.

memes.label: Index of Persons
memes.description: An index of all person pages in The Mirror of the Sea knowledge base — captains, mates, seamen, pilots, naval officers, and shipowners mentioned across Conrad's memoir.

memes.label: Pilot
memes.description: The local navigational expert who boards a ship to guide her through dangerous coastal waters — a figure of authority whose knowledge is specific, local, and irreplaceable.

relations.label: The Nursery of the Craft — XXXIX

memes.label: Index of Places
memes.description: An index of all place pages in The Mirror of the Sea knowledge base — ports, docks, seas, oceans, estuaries, coasts, and anchorages featured in Conrad's maritime recollections.

memes.label: Poop
memes.description: The raised aftermost deck of a ship, serving as the commander's station and vantage point from which the vessel is conned.

memes.label: Por el Rey
memes.description: A balancelle of the Carlist syndicate, used alongside the *Tremolino* for smuggling operations along the Mediterranean coast of Spain — her name, "For the King," a Carlist rallying cry that echoed through the syndicate's enterprises.

memes.label: Port
memes.description: The destination that gives meaning to a passage; in Conrad's vision the port is not merely a place but the punctuation mark that closes a period of a seaman's life.

memes.label: Quartermaster
memes.description: A senior seaman responsible for steering the ship, maintaining the helm, and overseeing deck operations related to navigation.

memes.label: Quay
memes.description: The stone edge of the shore where ships make fast, a boundary between land and water that Conrad renders with vivid particularity across the world's harbours.

memes.label: Rail
memes.description: The uppermost edge of a vessel's side — in Conrad's world, a vantage-point for command, a perch for reflection, and a boundary between the ship's order and the sea's chaos.

memes.label: Rigging
memes.description: The complex system of ropes, wires, and chains that support a ship's masts and control its sails — a web of lines that gives the sailing ship its characteristic silhouette and defines much of the seaman's daily labour.

memes.label: Royal
memes.description: The highest square sail set on a sailing ship's mast; in Conrad's memoir, the royal is a symbol of the ship's towering height and the daring required to handle canvas aloft in heavy weather.

memes.label: The Royal Navy
memes.description: The Royal Navy as it appears in The Mirror of the Sea — the great naval institution whose heroic age under Lord Nelson represented, for Conrad, the pinnacle of maritime character and whose spirit persisted, transmuted, into the age of steam.

memes.label: "Rulers of East and West — XXIX"
memes.description: "In the atmosphere of the Easterly Weather, as pellucid as a piece of crystal and refracting like a prism, we could see the appalling numbers of our helpless company, even to those who in more normal c..."

memes.label: "Rulers of East and West — XXV"
memes.description: "There is no part of the world of coasts, continents, oceans, seas, straits, capes, and islands which is not under the sway of a reigning wind, the sovereign of its typical weather. The wind rules the"

memes.label: "Rulers of East and West — XXVI"
memes.description: "The West Wind reigns over the seas surrounding the coasts of these kingdoms; and from the gateways of the channels, from promontories as if from watchtowers, from estuaries of rivers as if from poster..."

memes.label: "Rulers of East and West — XXVII"
memes.description: "Heralded by the increasing fierceness of the squalls, sometimes by a faint flash of lightning like the signal of a lighted torch waved far away behind the clouds, the shift of wind comes at last, the"

memes.label: "Rulers of East and West — XXVIII"
memes.description: "The winds of North and South are, as I have said, but small princes amongst the powers of the sea. They have no territory of their own; they are not reigning winds anywhere. Yet it is from their house..."

memes.label: Running
memes.description: The act of sailing with the wind astern — the ship's swiftest and most exhilarating point of sail, but also one that demands vigilance from those who stand her deck.

memes.label: Sail
memes.description: The fabric structure that catches the wind to propel a vessel, and the art of its handling — which Conrad regarded as a fine art already receding from the world, displaced by the mechanical certainties of steam.

memes.label: Sailing
memes.description: The art of propelling a vessel by wind power—Conrad elevates this practical skill to the status of a "fine art," requiring intuition, precision, and a lifelong devotion to craft.

memes.label: Savona
memes.description: An Italian port city on the Ligurian coast, whose river was the birthplace of the tartane *Tremolino* — Conrad's "cradle" in his Mediterranean years, built by a famous builder of boats and rigged in Corsica.

memes.label: Schooner
memes.description: A two-masted fore-and-aft rigged vessel; in Conrad's estimation, the finest type for long pleasure voyages and the most balanced in appearance at anchor.

memes.label: Seaman
memes.description: The professional sailor in Joseph Conrad's maritime world — a figure defined by practical skill, accumulated sea-wisdom, and a craft understood as a fine art rather than a mere trade.

memes.label: Seamanship
memes.description: The accumulated practical wisdom of the professional mariner — a body of knowledge that elevates competence into art and distinguishes the true seaman from the mere sailor.

relations.label: The Nursery of the Craft — XXXVII
The Nursery of the Craft — XXXVIII

memes.label: Sheet
memes.description: In sailing, a sheet is the line that controls the angle of a sail to the wind — hauled in to close-haul, eased out to run free. In Conrad's prose the word also carries its older sense of a broad surface or expanse of water, often to striking metaphorical effect.

memes.label: Ship Parts and Structure
memes.description: A thematic index of ship parts and structural components described in The Mirror of the Sea — from keel to truck, the physical anatomy of sailing vessels.

memes.label: Shipping Gazette
memes.description: The maritime newspaper whose columns chronicled the fate of ships — from "overdue" to "missing" — and served as the daily record of the merchant marine's triumphs and losses.

memes.label: Ship's Wake
memes.description: The visible trace a ship leaves on the water — Conrad's metaphor for the passage of time, the impermanence of experience, and the relationship between a vessel and the sea she has crossed.

memes.label: Skylight
memes.description: A raised window structure on a ship's deck that admits light and sometimes ventilation into the interior spaces below, particularly the officers' quarters and saloon.

memes.label: Snowstorm
memes.description: Among the enemies of a good Landfall, the snowstorm is the most disorienting — a white obliteration of sea and sky in which a ship may vanish for weeks, only to reappear as a ghostly apparition before the eyes of another vessel's crew.

memes.label: Southern Ocean
memes.description: The stormy circumpolar ocean south of the great capes, where the West Wind holds "autocratic sway" and where Conrad experienced some of the most violent weather of his seafaring life.

memes.label: Spanker
memes.description: The fore-and-aft sail set on the aftermost mast of a square-rigged ship, and its supporting boom — a spar vulnerable to collision in crowded anchorages.

memes.label: Spar
memes.description: A general term for any wooden or metal pole used to support sails and rigging aboard a sailing vessel — including masts, yards, booms, and gaffs.

memes.label: Squall
memes.description: A sudden, violent gust of wind, often accompanied by rain, hail, or snow — a phenomenon Conrad describes with the precision of a seasoned mariner and a recurrent dramatic engine in his maritime narratives.

memes.label: Steamer
memes.description: A ship propelled by steam power rather than sail — in Conrad's reckoning, a vessel whose mechanical heart and disdain for the sea mark a fundamental rupture in the ancient intimacy between the mariner and the element he navigates.

memes.label: Stern
memes.description: The rear or after part of a ship, where the vessel's name was traditionally displayed and where the captain's quarters were often located — a vantage point for contemplation and a marker of a ship's era and design philosophy.

memes.label: Steward
memes.description: The ship's domestic servant — in Conrad's memoir, a figure of irony and pathos who serves empty plates in a gale and absorbs the captain's ill-humours at the cabin table.

memes.label: Swell
memes.description: The long-period undulation of the open sea — a residual heave from distant winds that Conrad describes as both graceful and ominous.

memes.label: Sydney
memes.description: Sydney as Conrad knew it in the 1890s — the antipodean port whose Circular Quay harboured laid-up wool-clippers and where he served as night-watchman of a captive ship, an experience that shaped some of his most vivid character sketches.

memes.label: Taffrail
memes.description: The rail at the stern of a ship, a vantage point from which Conrad's captains and seamen watched the wake recede and weighed their thoughts.

memes.label: Tartane
memes.description: A small Mediterranean single-masted vessel with a lateen sail; Conrad's own *Tremolino* was described on her papers as a "tartane of sixty tons" — though in truth she was something else entirely.

memes.label: Thames Estuary
memes.description: The Thames Estuary as Conrad knew it — the approach to London from the sea, a transitional zone where the ocean surrenders to the river, and where every lightship, chimney, and dock tells the homeward-bound seaman that his voyage is ending.

memes.label: "The Character of the Foe — XXII"
memes.description: "It seems to me that no man born and truthful to himself could declare that he ever saw the sea looking young as the earth looks young in spring. But some of us, regarding the ocean with understanding"

memes.label: "The Character of the Foe — XXIII"
memes.description: "And so much depends upon the craft which, made by man, is one with man, that the sea shall wear for him another aspect. I remember once seeing the commander—officially the master, by courtesy the capt..."

memes.label: "The Character of the Foe — XXIV"
memes.description: "For, after all, a gale of wind, the thing of mighty sound, is inarticulate. It is a man who, in a chance phrase, interprets the elemental passion of his enemy. Thus there is another gale in my memory,..."

memes.label: "The Faithful River — XXX"
memes.description: "The estuaries of rivers appeal strongly to an adventurous imagination. This appeal is not always a charm, for there are estuaries of a particularly dispiriting ugliness: lowlands, mudflats, or perhaps..."

memes.label: "The Faithful River — XXXI"
memes.description: "The Nore sand remains covered at low-water, and never seen by human eye; but the Nore is a name to conjure with visions of historical events, of battles, of fleets, of mutinies, of watch and ward kept..."

memes.label: "The Faithful River — XXXII"
memes.description: "The view of ships lying moored in some of the older docks of London has always suggested to my mind the image of a flock of swans kept in the flooded backyard of grim tenement houses. The flatness of"

memes.label: "The Faithful River — XXXIII"
memes.description: "A ship in dock, surrounded by quays and the walls of warehouses, has the appearance of a prisoner meditating upon freedom in the sadness of a free spirit put under restraint. Chain cables and stout ro..."

memes.label: "The Faithful River — XXXIV"
memes.description: "These towns of the Antipodes, not so great then as they are now, took an interest in the shipping, the running links with “home,” whose numbers confirmed the sense of their growing importance. They ma..."

memes.label: "The Faithful River — XXXV"
memes.description: "“Ships!” exclaimed an elderly seaman in clean shore togs. “Ships”—and his keen glance, turning away from my face, ran along the vista of magnificent figureheads that in the late seventies used to over..."

memes.label: "The Faithful River — XXXVI"
memes.description: "The love that is given to ships is profoundly different from the love men feel for every other work of their hands—the love they bear to their houses, for instance—because it is untainted by the pride..."

memes.label: "The Grip of the Land — XX"
memes.description: "It is difficult for a seaman to believe that his stranded ship does not feel as unhappy at the unnatural predicament of having no water under her keel as he is himself at feeling her stranded. Strandi..."

memes.label: "The Grip of the Land — XXI"
memes.description: "That is why your “strandings” are for the most part so unexpected. In fact, they are all unexpected, except those heralded by some short glimpse of the danger, full of agitation and excitement, like a..."

memes.label: "The Nursery of the Craft — XXXIX"
memes.description: "The charm of the Mediterranean dwells in the unforgettable flavour of my early days, and to this hour this sea, upon which the Romans alone ruled without dispute, has kept for me the fascination of yo..."

memes.label: "The Nursery of the Craft — XXXVII"
memes.description: "The cradle of oversea traffic and of the art of naval combats, the Mediterranean, apart from all the associations of adventure and glory, the common heritage of all mankind, makes a tender appeal to a..."

memes.label: "The Nursery of the Craft — XXXVIII"
memes.description: "Happy he who, like Ulysses, has made an adventurous voyage; and there is no such sea for adventurous voyages as the Mediterranean—the inland sea which the ancients looked upon as so vast and so full o..."

memes.label: "The Tremolino — XL"
memes.description: "It was written that there, in the nursery of our navigating ancestors, I should learn to walk in the ways of my craft and grow in the love of the sea, blind as young love often is, but absorbing and d..."

memes.label: "The Tremolino — XLI"
memes.description: "We four formed (to use a term well understood nowadays in every social sphere) a “syndicate” owning the *Tremolino*: an international and astonishing syndicate. And we were all ardent Royalists of the..."

memes.label: "The Tremolino — XLII"
memes.description: "It was not *Tremolino*’s fault that the syndicate depended so much on the wit and wisdom and the information of Doña Rita. She had taken a little furnished house on the Prado for the good of the cause..."

memes.label: "The Tremolino — XLIII"
memes.description: "Anyway, he was perfect, as Doña Rita had declared. The only thing unsatisfactory (and even inexplicable) about our Dominic was his nephew, Cesar. It was startling to see a desolate expression of shame..."

memes.label: "The Tremolino — XLIV"
memes.description: "We slipped out to sea quietly at dusk, and all through the night everything went well. The breeze was gusty; a southerly blow was making up. It was fair wind for our course. Now and then Dominic slowl..."

memes.label: "The Tremolino — XLIX"
memes.description: "This the navy of the Twenty Years’ War knew well how to do, and never better than when Lord Nelson had breathed into its soul his own passion of honour and fame. It was a fortunate navy. Its victories..."

memes.label: "The Tremolino — XLV"
memes.description: "It was settled. I had now the courage to turn about. Our men crouched about the decks here and there with anxious, crestfallen faces, all turned one way to watch the chaser. For the first time that mo..."

memes.label: "The Tremolino — XLVI"
memes.description: "“A fellow has now no chance of promotion unless he jumps into the muzzle of a gun and crawls out of the touchhole.” He who, a hundred years ago, more or less, pronounced the above words in the uneasin..."

memes.label: "The Tremolino — XLVII"
memes.description: "The British navy may well have ceased to count its victories. It is rich beyond the wildest dreams of success and fame. It may well, rather, on a culminating day of its history, cast about for the mem..."

memes.label: "The Tremolino — XLVIII"
memes.description: "And yet the fact remains that, had the wind failed and the fleet lost steerage way, or, worse still, had it been taken aback from the eastward, with its leaders within short range of the enemy’s guns,..."

memes.label: "The Weight of the Burden — XIII"
memes.description: "There has been a time when a ship’s chief mate, pocketbook in hand and pencil behind his ear, kept one eye aloft upon his riggers and the other down the hatchway on the stevedores, and watched the dis..."

memes.label: "The Weight of the Burden — XIV"
memes.description: "The sailing-ship, when I knew her in her days of perfection, was a sensible creature. When I say her days of perfection, I mean perfection of build, gear, seaworthy qualities and ease of handling, not..."

memes.label: "The Weight of the Burden — XV"
memes.description: "So seemed to think the new captain, who arrived the day after we had finished loading, on the very eve of the day of sailing. I first beheld him on the quay, a complete stranger to me, obviously not a..."

memes.label: Tides
memes.description: The rise and fall of the sea — the governing rhythm of every port, departure, and arrival in coastal waters, tidal rivers, and the lower reaches of the Thames.

memes.label: Tilbury Docks
memes.description: The most modern of the London docks in Conrad's time, built on the Essex marshes downstream of the city — vast, isolated, and magnificent in their desolation, embodying the romance of efficiency awaiting its full purpose.

memes.label: Topgallant
memes.description: The third section of a square-rigged sailing ship's mast above the deck — high aloft, where the incongruous proximity of yardarms to warehouse windows becomes a London dock hazard.

memes.label: Trade Wind
memes.description: The steady easterly winds of the tropical belts — described by Conrad as serene monarchs of their intertropical kingdoms, essential to the merchant sailing routes.

memes.label: Maritime Tradition
memes.description: The accumulated body of seafaring practice, lore, and professional pride handed down through generations of mariners — which Conrad regarded as the unwritten constitution of the sea-going life.

memes.label: Trafalgar
memes.description: Cape Trafalgar on the Spanish coast, south of Cádiz, is the site of the most celebrated naval battle in British history, fought on 21 October 1805. For Conrad — a seaman writing half a century later — Trafalgar was more than geography: it was the sacred ground of the Royal Navy's professional memory, the day upon which the service measured its own soul.

memes.label: Tremolino
memes.description: The tartane Tremolino — a Mediterranean vessel of ancient design, built on the River of Savona and rigged in Corsica, which Conrad loved as the "cradle" of his young seafaring years and whose final, desperate voyage along the Catalan coast is the dramatic climax of The Mirror of the Sea.

memes.label: Trinity House
memes.description: The Corporation of Trinity House — the ancient maritime authority responsible for lighthouses, lightships, buoys, and pilotage in English waters, whose presence in The Mirror of the Sea is felt in every lights beam and channel buoy that guides the homeward-bound ship through the Thames Estuary.

memes.label: Trireme
memes.description: An ancient Mediterranean warship with three banks of oars--mentioned by Conrad in The Mirror of the Sea as the heavy warship that a Roman commander would not have brought into the shallow Thames estuary.

memes.label: Tweed
memes.description: The iron wool-clipper Tweed — a Clyde-built sailing ship aboard which Conrad served as a young officer and was unexpectedly promoted to officer of the watch, the vessel that taught him "what carrying on meant" and whose captain believed her speed lay in "the captain's right feeling for his ship."

memes.label: "Uncopyright"
memes.description: "Chapter of The Mirror of the Sea"

memes.label: Watch
memes.description: The four-hour division of shipboard duty — a rhythm that, in Conrad's rendering, defines the seaman's relationship with time, fatigue, the sea, and his shipmates, shaping consciousness itself through its unbroken cycle.

memes.label: Wind and Weather Terms
memes.description: A thematic index of wind and weather terminology in The Mirror of the Sea — the gales, breezes, squalls, and atmospheric phenomena that governed every voyage.

memes.label: West India Docks
memes.description: The great dock complex on the Isle of Dogs, which Conrad praises as the most "interesting" of the Thames docks — a monument to the West India trade that was, in its day, the largest dock enterprise in the world.

memes.label: West Indies
memes.description: The West Indies as they appear in Conrad's narrative — a distant trading region glimpsed through the prism of the sugar and rum trades, part of the vast geography of British maritime commerce.

memes.label: West Wind
memes.description: The prevailing wind of the North Atlantic — the greatest of the winds, personified by Conrad as a barbarian king whose moods range from tyrannical southwesterly fury to the star-jewelled clarity of his northwesterly phase.

memes.label: Westerly Weather
memes.description: The prevailing westerly winds of the high southern latitudes — a force of nature through which all great sailing passages must pass, and which Conrad treats as a sovereign power ruling the southern oceans.

relations.label: Rulers of East and West — XXVI
Rulers of East and West — XXVII

memes.label: Whaler
memes.description: A ship engaged in whaling--in The Mirror of the Sea, a whaler appears in the remote Southern Ocean as the accidental rescuer of a disabled steamer.

memes.label: Wind
memes.description: The elemental force that drives all sailing ships — Conrad's philosophical geography of the prevailing winds as sovereign rulers of the world's oceans, from the frank West Wind to the duplicitous East Wind.

relations.label: Rulers of East and West — XXV
Rulers of East and West — XXVI
Rulers of East and West — XXVII
Rulers of East and West — XXVIII
Rulers of East and West — XXIX

memes.label: Windlass
memes.description: The deck-mounted winch used to raise a ship's anchor — whose clanking chorus across the Downs was, for Conrad, the very sound of a ship coming to life.

memes.label: Windward
memes.description: The side of a vessel facing into the wind; in Conrad's maritime world, the exposed, wind-battered side from which squalls, gales, and the sea's fury approach.

memes.label: Index of Ships
memes.description: A comprehensive index of all ships and vessels referenced in The Mirror of the Sea, from Conrad's own commands to the ancient galleys and warships of Nelson's fleet.

memes.label: Yard
memes.description: A long spar on a sailing ship's mast from which sails are set — a defining feature of square-rigged vessels in Conrad's maritime world.

memes.label: Yawl
memes.description: A two-masted fore-and-aft rig ideal for cruising in home waters--"the yawl rig one comes in time to love," writes Conrad in his typology of sailing craft.
